title: Fitting a door draught excluder
subtitle: Fit a brush strip or threshold seal to stop cold air under an external door
excerpt: How to fit a draught excluder to the bottom of an external door. Covers choosing the right type of excluder, trimming the door if needed, and fitting the strip so it seals under the door without dragging on the floor.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional UK joinery practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: An aluminium-cased excluder strip fitted to the bottom of a door. It has a flexible rubber or brush insert that wipes against the threshold strip on the floor, forming a seal when the door is closed.

content.text: A 5 mm gap under an external door loses as much heat as leaving a small window open. The right type of excluder depends on the gap size and whether the door opens inward or outward, a strip that catches the floor when the door opens will strip itself off within weeks.
Step 1, Measure the gap and choose the type

Close the door and measure the gap between the door bottom and the floor at several points. For gaps up to 10 mm on an inward-opening door, a brush strip fixed to the door face works well. For gaps of 10 to 25 mm, a
that lifts when the door opens is more practical. For outward-opening doors, a threshold bar fitted to the sill is usually easier than a strip on the door.
Step 2, Trim the excluder to length

Most excluder strips come in a standard length of 900 mm or 1000 mm. Measure the door width and cut the strip to match using the tenon saw (for aluminium cases with a brush insert, a fine-toothed saw cuts cleanly; score the aluminium first with a utility knife before sawing). Deburr the cut end with the utility knife edge.

Hold the strip in position on the door face (for a brush strip, the brush bristles should just touch the floor when the door is closed). Mark the screw hole positions with a pencil. For a threshold seal, position it so the seal wipes the floor without lifting the door clear of the threshold.

Drill pilot holes at the marked positions (use a 2 mm bit for softwood door rails). Drive the screws to hold the strip firm without crushing the aluminium case. Open and close the door to confirm the strip seals without dragging. If the brush catches on a raised threshold or mat edge, trim the door bottom with the block plane to increase the clearance slightly.
Internal doors in older houses: an adhesive-backed foam strip in the door frame rebate fills the perimeter gap and silences the rattle. Easy to fit and replace.
Gaps on the sides or top of a door: fit a foam or rubber compression seal into the door stop rebate, pressing it in firmly so it compresses when the door closes.

items.symptom: Strip scrapes on the floor and the door will not open smoothly
items.cause: Strip positioned too low, or door bottom not high enough above the floor
items.fix: Raise the strip slightly by moving it up 2 to 3 mm on the door face. If the door bottom is too low, plane the door bottom to increase clearance before refitting.

items.symptom: Cold air still coming through after fitting
items.cause: Gap larger than the brush or seal can span, or strip twisted so the seal lifts at one side
items.fix: Check the strip is straight and fully pressed against the door face. For large gaps, fit a threshold seal rather than a brush strip.
